identified_property: severity of debt terms and creditor

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: You owe $100,000,000 to a violent criminal organization that charges compounding daily interest at 50% annually, and they have promised fatal consequences for you and your family if you miss a single payment.

variations.label: low
variations.text: You owe $100,000,000 to a ruthless loan shark who charges 30% annual compounding interest, has placed liens on everything you own, and employs enforcers who will cause you serious bodily harm for late payments.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: You owe $100,000,000 to an aggressive corporate creditor charging 18% annual interest, who will pursue relentless legal action, full wage garnishment, and complete asset seizure at the first missed payment.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: You owe $100,000,000 to a patient institutional lender at a low 3% fixed interest rate, with a flexible 30-year repayment plan and no penalties for occasional missed payments.

variations.label: high
variations.text: You owe $100,000,000 to a sympathetic family friend who charges zero interest, requires no collateral, and has told you to pay it back in whatever amounts you can manage, whenever you're able.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: You owe $100,000,000 to a wealthy relative who charges no interest, has no repayment deadline, has explicitly said not to stress about it, and has strongly hinted they intend to forgive the entire debt.

quality_checks.reasoning: All tiers describe owing $100,000,000, preserving the core scenario. The property varied is the severity of debt terms (interest rate, penalties, enforcement) and the nature of the creditor (violent criminals → loan shark → aggressive corporation → unspecified → patient lender → sympathetic friend → forgiving relative). Each step represents a meaningful, monotonic improvement in preferability.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a debt with no interest, no deadline, no enforcement, and a strong likelihood of total forgiveness is the mildest possible version of owing $100,000,000 — effectively approaching no real debt at all.
